metadata.title: Why the Recursion Tree Rows All Cost the Same
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = flat row cost / 2^j cancellation; CRIMSON = expected quadratic / naive fear
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no proof that merge costs 6m, no non-power-of-two extension, no sorting algorithm comparison. Wonder register.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Double the list, and merge sort barely more than doubles the work. The reason: every row of the recursion tree costs exactly the same amount.

card.copy: Why the Recursion Tree Rows All Cost the Same

beats.narration_text: Merge sort splits a list in half, sorts each half recursively, then merges. The merge of two lists of size m costs about 6m operations. Split, recurse, merge — that's the whole algorithm.

production_viz.label: Split → recurse → merge diagram; merge step labeled 6m; TEAL flow arrows

beats.narration_text: Draw the recursion tree for n equals 8. Row 0: one piece of size 8, merge cost 48. Row 1: two pieces of size 4, each costs 24, total 48. Row 2: four pieces of size 2, each costs 12, total 48. Every row is 48.

card.copy: Why does every row cost exactly 6n?

beats.narration_text: Why? Row j has 2^j pieces, each of size n over 2^j. Cost per piece: 6 times n over 2^j. Total for row j: 2^j times 6 times n over 2^j, which equals 6n. The 2^j pieces and 2^j denominator cancel. The row count grows; the row size shrinks; they cancel exactly.

production_viz.label: Row j formula: 2^j × 6 × (n/2^j) = 6n; cancellation highlighted in TEAL; row stamp showing flat 6n

beats.narration_text: This cancellation is the entire reason merge sort is O(n log n). The work per row is constant at 6n. The number of rows is log₂n — since n over 2^j reaches 1 when j equals log₂n. Total: 6n times log₂n, plus the base level.

production_viz.label: Full recursion tree for n=8; each row stamped '6n=48'; row count = log2(8)=3; total = 3×48

beats.narration_text: Now double n from 8 to 16. What changes? The same cancellation holds at each existing row — each still costs 6 times 16, which is 96. But there's one new row at the bottom. Total: 4 rows times 96 equals 384, which equals 6 times 16 times log₂16. Doubling n adds exactly one row.

production_viz.label: n=8 tree (3 rows, 48 each) vs n=16 tree (4 rows, 96 each); one new row highlighted in TEAL

beats.narration_text: Adding one row means adding 6n more operations. That's why the growth is n log n rather than n squared: each doubling adds a constant-cost row, not another factor of n.

production_viz.label: n log n curve vs n^2 curve; doubling adds one row label; TEAL=n log n, CRIMSON=n^2

beats.narration_text: For n equals 1024 — that's 10 rows: total approximately 6 times 1024 times 10, which is 61,440 operations. For n equals 2048 — 11 rows: approximately 135,168. The ratio is about 2.2. Doubling the input cost 2.2 times, not 4 times. This is the O(n log n) signature.

production_viz.label: n=1024: 61,440 ops. n=2048: 135,168 ops. Ratio=2.2×. Compare to 4× for n^2. TEAL=actual, CRIMSON=n^2 expectation

beats.narration_text: The same analysis applies to any divide-and-conquer algorithm where the merge step is linear. The tree structure ensures that the doubling and halving cancel — wider trees have shorter pieces, and the product stays flat.

production_viz.label: General divide-and-conquer tree; wider = shorter pieces; product stays flat at 6n per row; TEAL annotation

beats.narration_text: 2^j pieces times n over 2^j cost equals 6n per row. Log₂n rows. Total: 6n log₂n. Doubling n adds one row. The cancellation makes every row equal, and that makes the algorithm fast.
med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
